Overall:
The following processes might be effective for inactivating ricin under drinking water conditions: use of chlorine, hydrogen peroxide, ozone, permanganate, and ultraviolet irradiation.  These studies were based on responses in animals to determine effectiveness. 
Membrane separation by reverse osmosis was effective in field-unit demonstrations.  In bench-scale studies, chlorine's effectiveness varied as high doses and long contact times were effective but lower doses and contact times were not.
Use of atypical treatment processes may also remove or destroy ricin, including use of iodine, bromine, 4-diazophenyl-B-d-galactopyranoside, acetic acid, and urea, but conditions were genrally not those of drinking water treqtment.
